narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, your profile includes a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical 3.21 average—alongside an LSAT score of 34, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings in the middle of your school's distributions, and a middle-income family background, with plans to attend part-time. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 52.9% chance that you would fail it on your first try, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from past students' academic records, backgrounds, and outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your choice to attend part-time, which stands out because only about 7% of students in the dataset did so, and it often correlates with tougher paths to bar success in the model's view. Counterbalancing that, your LSAT score had the second-strongest influence, working in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, even though it's below the average of 36.7. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a meaningful boost toward a pass prediction, helping offset some concerns due to its strength relative to peers. Your high school decile rankings and family income played smaller roles overall, neither strongly tipping the balance either way. Compared to typical students who passed the bar, your part-time status is a bigger departure from the norm—most successful ones went full-time—while your GPA edges above average, giving you a foothold many lack. This close call at 52.9% shows how finely balanced your profile is, with just one key factor like enrollment status making the difference. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan if feasible, or retake the LSAT to push closer to or above 37 for a stronger positive signal. Building more evidence of your commitment, like additional academic achievements or bar prep experience, could also shift future predictions in your favor. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these standout areas head-on.
